coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient sandwich design thin film luminescent solar concentrators based on blue and green emissive MAPbBr3 perovskites nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Mahen, Ea Cahya Septia
Ummah, Auliya Rahmatul
Permatasari, Fitri Aulia
Suprijadi,
Aimon, Akfiny Hasdi
Iskandar, Ferry
coredata.dc:description: This study deals with the optimally designed blue and green emissive MAPbBr3 perovskite nanostructures (PNSs). Later PNSs were utilized as fluorescent material in a sandwich-design thin film luminescent solar concentrator (SD-TLSC). The fabricated SD-TLSC exhibits high performance with an impressive optical efficiency of 5.75% and a high average visible transmission (AVT) of 85.7%, which is the two-fold enhancement of the conventional single thin film LSC configuration. The high performance of the SD-TLC is induced by effective solar radiation absorption in the entire wavelength ranges, which is attributed to its unique morphology that exhibit different PL emission. These ground-breaking results pave the way for the potential practical use of perovskite-based SD-TLSC as solar windows, marking a major advancement in the field of building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoplates
Perovskite
Solar cells
Luminescent solar concentrators
Thin film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images of the colloidal (a) B-MAPbBr3 and (b) G-MAPbBr3. Absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of (c) colloidal B-MAPbBr3 and (d) G-MAPbBr3.
Absorption and PL spectra of the composite film: (a) B-MAPbBr3/PMMA; (b) G-MAPbBr3/PMMA. PL mapping of composite films: (c) B-MAPbBr3/PMMA; (d) G-MAPbBr3/PMMA.

Cross-sectional SEM images: (a) B-MAPbBr3/PMMA film; (b) G-MAPbBr3/PMMA film. (c) Digital photographed images. (d) Transmittance spectra of the as-prepared TLSC composed of B-MAPbBr3/PMMA, G-MAPbBr3/PMMA, SD-TLSC MAPbBr3.

(a) Photographed images of the LSC composed of B-MAPbBr3, G-MAPbBr3, and SD-TLSC MAPbBr3. (b) Schematic illustration of SD-TLSCs configuration. (c) The J-V curves of the Si solar cell integrated with TLSCs.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient sandwich design thin film luminescent solar concentrators based on blue and green emissive MAPbBr3 perovskites nanostructures

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study deals with the optimally designed blue and green emissive MAPbBr3 perovskite nanostructures (PNSs). Later PNSs were utilized as fluorescent material in a sandwich-design thin film luminescent solar concentrator (SD-TLSC). The fabricated SD-TLSC exhibits high performance with an impressive optical efficiency of 5.75% and a high average visible transmission (AVT) of 85.7%, which is the two-fold enhancement of the conventional single thin film LSC configuration. The high performance of the SD-TLC is induced by effective solar radiation absorption in the entire wavelength ranges, which is attributed to its unique morphology that exhibit different PL emission. These ground-breaking results pave the way for the potential practical use of perovskite-based SD-TLSC as solar windows, marking a major advancement in the field of building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSC) are devices containing luminous compounds that concentrated solar energy onto photovoltaic cells through the total internal reflections (TIR), enhancing the efficiency of the solar cell [1]. Compared to bulk-plate LSC, the thin film LSC (TLSC) has certain technical benefits since it may be deposited as a standard coating on any transparent substrate with adequate optical qualities, thereby expanding the utility of these devices, particularly in building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV) applications [2].

Over the last decades, various novel nanomaterials to improve the efficiency of TLSC devices have been explored, such as fluorescent organic dyes [3], CdSe/CdS [4], perovskite [5], carbon dots [6]. Among other nanoparticles, MAPbBr3 (MA = CH3NH3), showed excellent performances in terms of stability, low-temperature process, and tuneable emission wavelength, enabling effective reabsorption and enhancing the solar cells' efficiency [7,8].

Comparing the common configuration, e.g., tandem configuration, the sandwich design thin film LSC (SD-TLSC) could exhibit higher performance, owing to the reduced reabsorption loss and high transparency [9]. Guiju et al., designed the SD-TLSC based on the CdSe/CdS QDs, exhibiting an external optical efficiency (ηopt) of ∼2.95 % [10]. Then, Cai et al., utilized Carbon dots and CsPbBr3/CsPbI3 QDs in SD-TLSC with ηopt of 3.76 % [11]. High-efficiency SD-TLSC was plausibly attributed to the multi-emission entrapped, reducing reabsorption loss [10,11]. To the best of our knowledge, the utilization of the multi-emission MAPbBr3 perovskite in SD-TLSC devices has not been reported.

In this study, we utilized two different emissive MAPbBr3 perovskites into SD-TLSC. The well-designed emissive MAPbBr3 perovskites were prepared through ligand re-precipitation, covering the entire UV–vis solar spectrum area with high transparency, and providing highly efficient LSC devices. The results showed that the fabricated SD-TLSC has a high average visible transmission (AVT) of 85.7 % and high optical conversion efficiency of 5.75 %, which is preferable for BIPV utilization.

The blue and green-emitting MAPbBr3 perovskite nanoparticles (denoted as B-MAPbBr3 and G-MAPbBr3, respectively), were prepared through a ligand-assisted re-precipitation (LARP) method as reported in our previous works [7,12]. The perovskite/PMMA-based TLSC was fabricated using doctor blade techniques onto the glass substrate. The prepared perovskite/PMMA-based TLSC was utilized as a solar concentrator on commercial silicon solar cells system. The detailed synthesis procedure, as well as characterization, were provided in Supplementary Information.

The morphological and optical properties of the as-prepared B-MAPbBr3 and G-MAPbBr3 perovskite nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 1 . B-MAPbBr3 are spherical with an average diameter of 5.7 ± 1.2 nm (Fig. 1a). While the G-MAPbBr3 in nanoplatelets shape with an average lateral dimension of 9.6 ± 1.8 nm (Fig. 1b). The morphological of the as-prepared MAPbBr3 were tunning by nucleation and growth process of perovskite nanostructure, dictated by the ratio ligand of oleylamine/oleic acid [13]. As consequence, the optical properties (both emission and absorption spectra) were also tunned through a quantum confinement effects [7,14]. The B-MAPbBr3 has an absorption edge at 450 nm and an emission peak at 467 nm (Fig. 1c), whereas the G-MAPbBr3 has an absorption edge at 515 nm and an emission peak at 512 nm (Fig. 1d). These well-designed optical spectra of the as-prepared MAPbBr3 that will be utilized in the LSC was expected enhance their performances, via reduction of the reabsorption loss caused by entrapped emissive light [10].

As a solar concentrator, the thin film consisting of B-MAPbBr3 /PMMA and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA composite films was prepared through a doctor blade technique with the optical properties of these films as shown in Fig. 2 . The B-MAPbBr3/PMMA composite film has an absorption edge at 463 nm and a PL peak position at 478 nm (Fig. 2a), whereas the G-MAPbBr3/PMMA composite film has a UV–vis absorption edge at 520 nm and a position of PL peak at 525 nm (Fig. 2b). Concerning their colloidal solution, a slight redshift both of PL emission and absorption spectra were observed on the as-prepared films. This optical evolution of MAPbBr3 from colloidal to thin film formation was ascribed to the alteration of the composite energy levels due to the PMMA dielectric screening effect [12]. The emission spectra of the B-MAPbBr3 /PMMA and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA composite films are excitation wavelength-independent (Fig. 2c-d), which is preferable for the LSC applications.

By optimizing the thin film fabrication, three TLSC devices, i.e., B-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC, G-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC, and sandwich design TLSC (SD-TLSC) were evaluated, as shown in Fig. 3 . The SD-TLSC configuration consists of the B-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC on each side of the glass substrate. B-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA TLSC possess an identical thickness of as much as 10.9 µm and 10.5 µm, respectively (Fig. 3a-b). All the as-prepared TLSCs show high transparency (Fig. 3c). Average visual transmission (AVT) and CIE color coordinates are two pivotal parameters for LSC utilization, especially in building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV) applications. The AVT calculation was explained in detail in the Supplementary Information. The AVT value derived from the transmission spectra of TLSC (Fig. 3d) for B-MAPbBr3, G-MAPbBr3, and SD-TLSC are 91.0 %, 88.7 %, and 85.7 %, respectively. The CIE 1931 color coordinates for B-MAPbBr3, G-MAPbBr3, and SD-TLSC MAPbBr3 are (0.32327, 0.3473); (0.32282, 0.341031), and (0.3338, 0.35647), respectively (Fig. S1). These coordinates are identical to the incident AM 1.5G color coordinates according to CIE 1931. These LSC exhibits exceptionally transparent with high AVT value and are close to the CIE 1931 incident AM 1.5G color coordinates indicating a potential application as a solar window in the BIPV applications.

The SD-TSLC configuration and performance were shown in Fig. 4 . The B-MAPbBr3/PMMA and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA thin film were homogeneously deposited on the glass substrate (Fig. 4a). The SD-TSLC were assembly by placing the B-MAPbBr3/PMMA film on the top layer and G-MAPbBr3/PMMA on bottom layers (Fig. 4b). At the top and edges, LSCs based on B-MAPbBr3/PMMA film emitted bright blue emissions. The bottom layer absorbs the cyan-green region of the solar spectrum and the remaining UV-violet solar spectrums that pass through the top layer and exits the photon with green emissions. In addition, the bottom layer of SD-TLSC also absorbs escaped blue emitted photons due to the escape cone from the glass substrate. Thus, all the emitted photons trapped inside LSC through TIR are guided to the PV cell positioned on the edge. The LSC performance was evaluated by the optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) and power conversion efficiency (ηPCE) [15]. The efficiency calculation was derived from the J-V photoelectric curve (Fig. 4c) and was explained in detail in Supplementary Information. The TLSC based on B-MAPbBr3, G-MAPbBr3, and SD-TLSC exhibited an optical conversion efficiency (ηopt) of 2.52 %, 4.12 %, and 5.75 %, respectively. While the power conversion efficiency (ηpce) corresponds to 0.92 %, 1.25 %, and 2.04 % for TLSC-based on B-MAPbBr3, G-MAPbBr3, and MAPbBr3-based SD-TLSC, respectively. It shows that the MAPbBr3 -based SD-TLSC exhibits twofold enhancement in both optical and power conversion efficiency with high transparency than the conventional TLSC.

In conclusion, we have designed highly efficient transparent MAPbBr3 perovskite-based SD-TLSCs. Two distinguish emissive MAPbBr3 perovskite nanostructures were employed as the top and bottom layers in sandwich design TLSC, providing a twofold efficiency than the conventional single emissive MAPbBr3 perovskite TLSC. Furthermore, the MAPbBr3 perovskite-based SD-TLSCs demonstrated high transparency with an AVT value of 85.7 %. This study suggested that the MAPbBr3 perovskite-based SD-TLSCs has promising potential application as solar window or BIPV application.

Ea Cahya Septia Mahen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Auliya Rahmatul Ummah: Investigation, Methodology. Fitri Aulia Permatasari: Writing – review & editing. Suprijadi: Writing – review & editing. Akfiny Hasdi Aimon: Writing – review & editing. Ferry Iskandar: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The Ministry of Education, Culture, Research and Technology through Doctoral Dissertation Research Program Grant No: 2/E1/KP.PTNBH/2021. E. C. S. Mahen thanks to Ministry of Religion Affairs Indonesia for the MORA scholarship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134008.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
